perturbation.original_text: The Client agrees to ensure that each of the Authorised Recipients adheres to the terms set forth in this NDA and acknowledges that it shall be liable for any damages incurred in connection with the breach of its obligations.
perturbation.changed_text: All correspondence, inquiries, offers to purchase and negotiations relating to the Transaction will be conducted exclusively through Vladi Private Islands. The Client and Authorised Recipients agree only to contact the property owner and/or the affiliates of the property owner through Vladi Private Islands . The Client agrees to ensure that each of the Authorised Recipients adheres to the terms set forth in this NDA and acknowledges that it shall be liable for any damages incurred in connection with the breach of its obligations.
perturbation.explanation: By moving the clause about the client's liability for breaches by authorized recipients into the section dealing with correspondence, it obscures the direct link between the core confidentiality obligations and the liability. It could be argued that the liability clause only applies to breaches related to correspondence and not the overall NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 278 of the German Civil Code (BGB)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 278 of the German Civil Code (BGB)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 278 BGB stipulates that a party is responsible for the fault of its agents. Relocating the liability clause makes it less clear that the client is fully responsible for all breaches of the NDA by authorized recipients, potentially weakening the enforceability of this provision under German law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: f restoration does not occur in good time; the claim to restoration is excluded. table of contents Section 251 Damages in money without the specification of a period of time (1) To the extent that restoration is not possible or is not sufficient to compensate the obligee, the person liable in damages is to compensate the obligee in money. (2) The person liable in damages may compensate the obligee in money if restoration is possible only at disproportionate expense. Expenses incurred as a result of the curative treatment of an injured animal are not disproportionate merely because they significantly exceed the value of the animal. table of contents Section 252 Lost profits The damage to be compensated also comprises the profits lost. Those profits are considered lost that in the normal course of events or in the special circumstances, particularly due to the measures and precautions taken, could be expected to be attained as a matter of likelihood. table of contents Section 253 Intangible damage (1) Money may be demanded in compensation for any damage that is not a pecuniary loss only in the cases stipulated by law. (2) If damages are to be paid for an injury to body, health, freedom or sexual self-determination, then equitable compensation in money also may be demanded for any damage that is not a pecuniary loss. table of contents Section 254 Contributory negligence (1) Where fault on the part of the injured person contributed to the occurrence of the damage, liability in damages as well as the extent of compensation to be provided depend on the circumstances, in particular on the extent to which the damage was caused mainly by one or the other party. (2) This also applies if the fault of the injured person is limited to failing to draw the attention of the obligor to the danger of unusually extensive damage, where the obligor neither was aware of the danger nor ought to have been aware of it, or to failing to avert or mitigate the damage. The provision of section 278 applies accordingly. table of contents Section 255 Assignment of claims to compensation A person who is to pay damages for the loss of a thing or a right is obliged to compensate only in return for the assignment of the claims which the person entitled to damages holds against third parties on the basis of ownership of the thing or on the basis of the right. table of contents Section 256 Payment of interest on expenses A person who is obliged to reimburse expenses is to pay interest on the amount expended from the date of the expense onwards or, if other objects than money have been expended, on the amount payable as compensation for their value. Where expenses have been incurred on an object that is to be returned to the person liable in damages, interest need not be paid for the period of time for which the person entitled to damages is unremunerated for the emoluments or fruits of the object. table of contents Section 257 Claim to release A person who is entitled to demand reimbursement of expenses they incur for a specific purpose may demand release, if they assume an obligation for this purpose, from the obligation. If the obligation is not yet due, then the person liable in damages may provide security to the person entitled instead of releasing them from the obligation. table of contents Section 258 Right of removal Anyone entitled to remove an installation from a thing that they are to return to another person is to restore the thing, in the event of removal, to its previous condition at their own expense. If the other person obtains possession of the thing, then that other person is obliged to permit the installation to be removed; they may refuse permission until they have been provided with security for the damage connected to the removal. table of contents Section 259 Extent of duty to render accounts (1) A person who is obliged to render accounts for management related to earnings or expenses is to provide the person entitled with accounts containing a
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Wolters Kluwer Note: Since your browser does not support JavaScript, you must press the Resume button once to proceed.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces a structural flaw by relocating the liability clause, potentially obscuring its application to all NDA breaches and weakening its enforceability. The original text clearly states the client is liable for breaches by authorized recipients. The altered text, by placing this liability within the context of correspondence, introduces ambiguity. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Section 278 BGB, it is not directly quoted and does not explicitly state liability for agents. However, the Law Explanation states Section 278 BGB stipulates that a party is responsible for the fault of its agents, indicating a legal basis for the client's liability for the actions of Authorised Recipients. The relocation of the liability clause makes it less clear that the client is fully responsible for *all* breaches of the NDA by authorized recipients, potentially weakening the enforceability of this provision.

perturbation.original_text: All correspondence, inquiries, offers to purchase and negotiations relating to the Transaction will be conducted exclusively through Vladi Private Islands. The Client and Authorised Recipients agree only to contact the property owner and/or the affiliates of the property owner through Vladi Private Islands .

The Client and Authorised Recipients agree only to contact the property owner and/or the affiliates of the property owner through Vladi Private Islands .
perturbation.explanation: By relocating a clause about contact with the property owner to below the signature block, there is an ambiguity as to whether the client agreed to it.
perturbation.law_explanation: BGB § 133 states that in interpreting a declaration of intent, the true intention shall be ascertained rather than adhering to the literal meaning of the expression. Placing a substantive obligation after the signature block implies it was not part of the agreed terms, potentially undermining the enforceability of this clause.

perturbation.justification: The altered text presents a structural flaw that undermines the enforceability of the agreement. By positioning the clause concerning exclusive contact with Vladi Private Islands *after* the signature block, a reasonable argument can be made that the client did not explicitly agree to this term. This is supported by the LLM's law explanation, which cites BGB § 133, emphasizing that legal interpretation should focus on 'the true intention' rather than a strict literal reading. Placing a crucial obligation after the signature implies it was not part of the intended agreement. Scraped law snippet 1 provides the full text of BGB § 133, which states: 'Bei der Auslegung einer Willenserklärung ist der wirkliche Wille zu erforschen und nicht an dem buchstäblichen Sinne des Ausdrucks zu haften,' reinforcing the need to ascertain the true intent behind the declaration.
